speaker: interviewer
text: --four. My conversation with Marianne Rosner, or Manci. Manci, your-- we left off where you were telling us that you arrived in the United States. And do you remember the date that you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: 24Th of May, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened at that point, when you arrived here?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened at that point? That was the-- it was the most-- most beautiful day in my life, I imagine. My brother-in-law-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --waited for us on the pier. And he-- he had a room for us, prepared in Astoria, in Queens. And my husband couldn't get, right away, work. He had to belong to the musician union in order to get a job. But I got a letter from a young-- a young boy. When I was in Bremerhaven, a young boy saw me in the train. And he said to me, you go to New York, and I have an aunt in New York. Would you-- I don't know the address, but I know he lives in Brooklyn. Maybe you can find it in the phone book or something. Maybe you could give--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took this letter. And I said to my brother-in-law, I have here a letter from a young boy. He lost his parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: He has his aunt here. I would like to-- I didn't read English. I didn't know a word of English.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took a phone book, a Brooklyn phone book. And I found this name, of this woman. Believe it or not, the first woman-- the first telephone call, I think-- was I found this woman. And I told her in-- in Polish-- and this time I spoke, already, good Polish-- I have a letter. Oh, she says, Mrs. Rosner, can I come and pick up the letter? I says-- I said, of sure-- of course. So she came the next day. And she was so happy. She says to me, Mrs. Rosner, what can I do for you to thank you, that you brought me this letter? I said, I'm in America. I don't need anything. The only thing I need is I need some work, so I can make some money. So she said well-- I said, I'm a dressmaker. So she went to the telephone and she called up a man, Mr. [INAUDIBLE], on Broadway, which had a dress shop. And he said, I am here with a lady which just came from Europe, and she needs some work. You have to give her some work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, let her come to me. And I started in this store as an alteration hand. I made $29 a week. It was beautiful. And I-- I used to work there for a certain time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I had a friend here from my childhood. He was in the fur line. And he said to me, you know what, Manci? When you can sew as a furrier, you can make much more money than as a dressmaker.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, really? Yes. So I found here, my husband had a customer in Warsaw, a furrier, which had a fur salon in-- in New York. So we went to see this Mr. [? Applebaum, ?] [? Maximilian. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, you have to belong to the furrier union. So I-- in order to work for me, I can hire only union people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but I give you a letter to the union, that you were working for me in Warsaw. And he gave me this letter. And I went to the union. And they accepted me in the union.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, after a few months, I got a job there, where-- and that's how I-- I worked for this man-- already he died. In the meantime, his sister took over-- 37 years. From 1947, till 1984, till I retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband, did he find-- did he finally join the union?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband joined the union. Before six month, they took him in. And he got a job. His first job was on Second Avenue in New York, in a nightclub called Russian [INAUDIBLE]. And he had to have a Russian name. They gave him the name of [? Gregori ?] [? Schumski. ?] That's how he started to work. But he didn't want to work as [? Gregori ?] [? Schumski. ?] So he looked for another job, where he could use his own name. Then he got together with his brother. His brother was a pianist. And they got together. They worked in a Polish restaurant, Polonaise, for 12 years. Then my-- my-- my brother-in-law's health, the children health start to become no good. They move to Arizona. So my-- my husband got some other other piano player. And he worked. And the last 15 years, he worked in the Sign of the Dove, a very, very fine restaurant in New York. Till he retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me a story about your husband's name, changing the-- the first name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me that again? He decided not to have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he-- he-- he-- he-- he was born-- in his birth certificate, he is Hermann. But he hated this name. Well, then especially when the-- when the German Hermann Goering, he couldn't stand. But in the Jewish tradition, his father's name was Henry. So he couldn't, as-- he couldn't take the same name. But then when his father was killed during the war, so when we came to America, he officinally-- when he-- when we changed-- when we got American citizens, he became Henry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- how long did you live in New York? Or-- and do you still live there now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are still live in New York. All the time, in New York. I love New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you come here how often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four months a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Hallandale?

speaker: interviewee
text: December, January, February, March. And we bought this apartment 10 year-- when we retired. We bought this apartment 10 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the children you have, and Alexander, and your great-grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two-- my husband-- my-- my son is-- he's married twice. With his first wife, he has two children, two boys, Gregory and Benjamin. And with his second wife, he has a little girl. Little girl, she's already 13 years old, Alexandra. And Benjamin, my oldest grandson, has a little boy, Simon, which is going to be, next month, two years old. He's my great-grandson. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we are-- there was-- I have a picture where there are four generation of the Rosners. You mentioned to me earlier, Manci, about Oskar Schindler, and the-- the filming of Steven Spielberg's movie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were involved in that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- how did that come to be? And what were your feelings?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when-- when the-- when Keneally wrote the book, you know? So, of course, he-- it took him two years to interview all these people, to get the story togethers. And that's how he came, also, to New York, to interview us, talk to us. And then one-- one-- one blue-- one day I got a phone call from California, from the Spielberg office, that Mr. Spielberg like to invite us to come to Jerusalem, to participate on the-- on the end of-- to finish up the film. So-- so I said, that I'll go only-- We were already elderly people. This is only a few years ago. My husband was already over 80 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, I go only when my son goes. Of course your son goes. And my son and his wife, and Henry and I, we went to Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Henry and I and Alexander, we were-- we had parts in the film. We were played by Polish actors, Henry as the musician. A matter of fact, Mr. Spielberg was so nice. He cutted out a scene from the film where Henry played on a party by Goth, where one-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --German officer committed suicide. And-- but Henry played a song, 'Sad Sunday,' 'Last Sunday,' in Polish. And he played this song. Well, this was a song where people used to commit suicide. Very sentimental song, with the words that people-- and Henry claims-- he claimed all the time-- that he had some kind of a power. Some kind of a suggestive power that when he played, he played this song for this guy, for this German maybe 10 times or 12 times in a row, over and over again and again and again, until this guy, he hypnotized him. He went out on the patio. And he took out his [? arrow ?] [? and he shot ?] him. And he had filmed that. But somehow they cut it out. But they sended me this cutout. I have at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were-- you actually took part in the film-- the filming, then, in Jerusalem?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure, as a blockalteste, Henry, as a musician. Alex, as a little-- little boy. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you actually were laying the stones, also, on--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, then-- then-- then we-- he all got us together. And we are then with our-- together made these-- I went with the actress. He went with the-- a matter of fact, I got a card from her for Christmas. We went around the grave. We laid stone on the grave, Oskar's grave. He's buried in the-- in the cemetery for the Christian people in-- in-- in Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your feelings about that whole event?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very emotional. Very emotional. And we were treated like kings and queens from Mr. Spielberg. Absolutely out of this world. This guy's such a beautiful human being, that it's unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of message do you hope that your story, telling me today, will give to other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there is only one message, to me, in my estimation. One message, it should never happen again. That people should stop hating each other. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There should be no-- no hate towards the Jews or hate towards the Christian or hates towards the Black or hates towards the Reds. There should be love between. This is the only message. The only message. And peace on earth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you, Manci, for your--

speaker: interviewee
text: And God bless America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for your testimony. And it's very valuable. And for your time, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This was taken here in Miami, in Hallandale, in my apartment, in connection for a-- for a magazine or a newspaper, in connection with

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, myself. We used, for fun, to play a little bit together. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is about, I would say, maybe three, four years ago. Something like this. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my younger grandson, Gregory, and his wife, [? Kim Adelle. ?] On their wedding in New York, in 1995. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my older grandson, Benjamin, and his wife, Anita, on their wedding, in New York, in 1992. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my granddaughter, Alexandra, when she was a little girl. Now she is-- she's going to be, in May, 13 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- on the left is my husband, Henry, 90 years old, holding his great-grandson, Simon. I think in this time he was maybe a year old. Next to him is my son, Alexander. And next to him is my grandson, the father of Simon, Benjamin. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is Mr. Stephen Spielberg and myself in Jerusalem, finishing up the film
